title: Electrical — Contactor and Capacitor Diagnosis
complaint: Outdoor unit not running. Indoor unit blows unconditioned air.
initialData: Thermostat calling for cooling — Y terminal has 24V
Outdoor unit completely off — no fan, no compressor

s1.prompt: Outdoor unit completely off, 24V at Y. What do you check first?

options.label: Check for 24V at the contactor coil and line voltage at the contactor

s1-wrong.prompt: The outdoor unit is completely off — no electrical components are running. Trace the electrical circuit before checking refrigerant or replacing parts.

s2.prompt: You measure: 24V at contactor coil terminals ✓, 240V line voltage at contactor line side ✓, 0V at contactor load side. What does this mean?

options.label: The contactor coil has 24V but contacts are not closing — failed contactor

s2-wrong.prompt: 24V is present at the coil (thermostat is calling). 240V is present at the line side (breaker is on). 0V at load side means the contacts are not closing.

s2-partial.prompt: A failed capacitor would not prevent the contactor from closing. The contactor has power but is not passing it through — the contacts are failed.

s3.prompt: You inspect the contactor — the contacts are severely pitted and burned. Before replacing, what else should you check?

options.label: Check the capacitor and compressor windings — pitted contacts often indicate a hard-starting compressor

s3-wrong.prompt: Refrigerant charge is not related to pitted contacts. Pitted contacts are often caused by a hard-starting compressor drawing excessive current.

s3-partial.prompt: Replacing the contactor alone may work, but pitted contacts often indicate a hard-starting compressor. Check the capacitor and windings to find the root cause.

options.label: Check capacitor and compressor windings first

pass.prompt: Correct. Failed contactor contacts prevented power from reaching the outdoor unit. Pitted contacts indicate a hard-starting compressor — check the capacitor and consider a hard-start kit.
pass.feedback: Pitted contactor contacts are often caused by a weak capacitor or hard-starting compressor drawing excessive inrush current. Fix the root cause, not just the symptom.
